author: crunchybrewski
content: Not entirely sure what you mean by pair. Typically in this room at least, Tony and JDub preach to compare the individual ticker to QQQ or SPY which represent general market sentiment. So you want to look for long positions when qqq is strong, and short when qqq is weak. There are cases where there are high timeframe objectives where that is ignored. But generally that is the case. Another critical part of the break and retest strategy is "large displacement" from the range. It looks to me like NFLX and ZOOM had some amount of displacement that seems reasonable to try a trade. One of those would have worked while the other wouldnt. 50% is still a profitable outcome with a 2R trade.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: you took short where i went long. My setup is retest though. Opening print is the first 1 min candle so you don't seem to have it marked correctly. I've done backtesting on this setup and in long run it works great. If it's above 1 min opening range i look for call. Below it, i look for puts. If you have a reversal setup you need to have clear understanding of it and play it at least from key levels (in the long run reversal never worked for me so i don't play it) But that 481.64 level i had from Wednesday low would have been a good shot at entry for reversal.

author: Papa
content: Sorry that this trade didn't work out for you.  From what I can see price did not confirm continuation to the downside, ie need to make sure it closes below PDL for a B/R.  That did not occur at the time of your trade entry, the candle you highlighted does not have good price action for the direction of your trade.  META in premarket was inside the prior day so you don't really want to take a trade until it breaks the PDL with displacement on a B/R if your trade thesis was to the downside.  Also keep in mind that the premarket levels are liquidity zones as well, so they usually need to be tested/swept.  Hope this makes sense.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Good Morning Sam - 

To answer your question :  Reversal Confirmation:

I observed a strong bullish rejection around the bottom of a clear demand zone where the price had a previous imbalance. The downward trendline broke as a reversal candle formed with a long lower wick, signaling buyers stepping in.
Following the rejection, a small bullish engulfing candle further confirmed momentum shifting to the upside.
I entered the trade where I labeled "Bought Apple Contracts", as the candles began printing higher lows and the downtrend lost steam.

Stop-Loss Placement:

I placed my stop-loss below the demand zone and the wick of the rejection candle. This aligned with key support and offered a good risk-to-reward ratio while protecting against further downside if the reversal failed.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Not sure of there's right/wrong answer for this

But if your ticker fails to go higher than yHOD, then you could use it as BnR level for going short, however I'd wait until it takes yLOD for having a better quality setup, because then you will trading internal liquidity (scalping) instead of waiting for external liquidity and a bigger move

And you need to be careful as the 1min/5min could look like a failed attempt but the 15min/1hr could be telling a different story (Price is fractal)

This is exactly what Jack said in the Playbook and A+ setups video about aligning all the timeframes for your move. Or said differently, you don't short in the smaller timeframe a ticker than has an uptrend in the HTF

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@1025807331327475863> 

Great question! Comparing your second chart and my first one, here’s how I’d approach the scenarios you outlined:

Scenario 1 (Green Arrow):
If NVDA filled the gap, broke the 5-minute opening range, retested the bottom line of the opening range, and then pushed up:

Yes, I would consider entering after the retest and confirmation of strength (e.g., a bullish candle closing above the retest level).
This setup aligns with gap-fill momentum, which often leads to a continuation if volume supports it.
Stop Loss (SL): Just below the retest level or slightly under the gap-fill zone to account for noise.
Scenario 2 (Red Arrow):
If NVDA broke the top of the 5-minute opening range, retested, and then started selling:

No, I wouldn’t enter here. The initial breakdown of the top of the range would signal weakness, especially if it couldn’t hold the breakout level.
Entering short after the breakdown could work for a different strategy, but for me, I’d wait for further confirmation that the sellers are in control before considering a trade in this direction.
Entry at 8:19 (Orange Arrow):

I entered because NVDA was forming a bullish triangle (as shown in my chart), signaling consolidation with the likelihood of a breakout. The price was respecting key levels like the premarket high and hourly resistance.
The breakout was accompanied by increasing volume, confirming strength.
SL for this trade: Below the last low of the triangle (around $138.51 on my chart), allowing the price room to breathe while managing risk effectively.
The primary difference is context—whether NVDA respects its key levels (gap-fill, opening range, premarket high) and how volume supports the move. Let me know if that helps clarify!

author: Angel (az)
content: As the others pointed out, the 5m was incorrectly marked so the retest was different, but I think I see what you were looking at, there was an immediate retest. It's to the downside, we seem to be breaking outside of consolidation after many days and below PDL right into the open with immediate retest. I would say this is quite an aggressive play, because it happens a few minutes into market open. The other comments I have (I make this same mistake myself) - you're taking it very close to LOD, so it is riskier. Ultimately, it did give you a new LOD at 9:42 where you should've taken some off. Thesis checks out for me, but the other factor was QQQ was slowly moving up, and I believe Jack and Tony said in the live that it would be hard to short. I think this is why it hit your targets ultimately, but it was very choppy on 1 minute. 15 minute retest of PML looks nice in hindsight.

author: Angel (az)
content: RR won't work out if you set your stop loss wider, I think it's always better to just re-enter on 84%. Also, we do have a lot more choppier PA recently, so it's better to be more cautious. I also see that you're entering a short off the top of 5 minute OR in the first image? You want to wait for it to get under the low of the OR for the short side. Second image I don't see what level you have B+R on, it doesn't look like a retest to me.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Trade Recap – QQQ (March 6, 2025)
Morning Chop: Multiple BNRs failed, no clean setups, stayed out.
Entry: Short after break & retest of key level.
Trend Confirmation: Strong rejection, followed the downtrend.
Trade Management: Scaled out at TP1 & TP2, respected trendline.
Exit: Closed all remaining contracts when price broke the PYA (Protect Your Asset) trendline.
Key Takeaways:

✅ Avoided early chop – waited for a solid setup.
✅ Break & retest gave conviction for the short.
✅ Trendline guided trade – locked profits before reversal.

author: unnas
content: I used to enter into these kind of trades when I was first learning about the break and retest strategy.  Every time I saw a good retest, I would take the trade (good thing it was only paper money back then).  The biggest lesson I learned is to develop a HTF thesis first, then enter a set up that lines up with the HTF thesis.  For example, we are currently in a downtrend and most stocks (including NVDA) are in a downtrend.  Therefore, a suggested setup would be to look for a shorting position on a key level.  I make sure to watch Jdubs premarket prep videos to get an idea of what to expect and what key levels to keep and eye on.  Trying to a Long position on a stock that is downtrending in the HTF is pretty risky.  Also, something new that I'm just learning is to use the QQQ and SPY price action to determine relative strength and weakness for the stock I'm considering to trade.  In a nut shell, the break and retest strategy works best when you pair it up with multiple confluences like HTF thesis, current market direction, and relative strength/weakness.  I'm very new to trading, but what' I'm learning so far is that simply applying the break and retest to a key level isn't optimal.  We need to start line up the strategy with multiple confluences.

author: thamerb777
content:
Thanks.
-Tuesday: Neutral. No direct impact from the Richmond Index, but it might move with QQQ. Hold off on trades until closer to earnings on Thursday.
-Wednesday: Markets may start positioning for Thursday’s earnings. If PMIs are strong and Waller is dovish, consider a small long position (calls) ahead of earnings. If data is weak, stay neutral or hedge (puts).
-Thursday: Avoid trading during the day unless economic data significantly impacts tech sentiment (e.g., strong Durable Goods Orders might support a pre-earnings rally). Post-earnings, if GOOGL beats (EPS > $2.01) and provides strong AI/Cloud guidance, go long for Friday. If it misses, short the initial drop or buy a dip if the long-term outlook remains solid.
-Friday: Post-earnings reaction dominates. If GOOGL beat expectations, expect a gap up—ride the momentum with a long position. If it missed, short the drop or buy a dip if the outlook is strong. Strong Consumer Sentiment could further support GOOGL.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi. Going Long very near the HOD is always with a great risk of reversal or a serious pullback. Its similar to going short near the LOD. author: jest1
content:
Hey Chris, just looking through this channel and saw this post from a couple of days ago about the 2 different green candles. I nerd out on technicals and wanted to offer an explanation (bear with the length of the reply)

While both candles show buyer strength and potential bullish reversal (especially at the bottom of a downtrend), the second candle is considered stronger. 

It's based on the wicks. The 2nd candle is a hammer (small body at the top of a long lower wick and little/no upper wick)

The long lower wick shows the stock traded (significantly) lower than its opening price during the candle period (I don't know what length of time is being shown) but buyers rallied to close on top, so there was buying pressure during the candle period to push price up.

The first candle isn't quite an inverted hammer (for hammers the wick should be ~2x the length of the body), but the "wick tells the story" part as we say here shows that buyers were initially strong and tried to push price up but couldn't close at those higher prices. Sellers pushed price back down, leaving the upper wick.

author: Royal191
content:
Hey <@268284125897687041> At a high level view, I would say slow down and focus on 1-2 trades per day. If you took 2 Ls there is no need to continue and possibly end up with 4 or 5 Ls. Just call it a day and review what went wrong and come back the next day with a fresh view. Overtrading and revenge trading is a big reason why traders are not profitable because your losing days become bigger than your winning days.
Based on what you said it seems like you might be chasing trades or anticipating moves. You should spend some time and refine your system rather than trying out different strategies or entry models. (if you haven’t watched the accelerator videos yet, I would highly recommend you do so).
Also based on your charts it looks like some of your trades are kinda taken in the middle of no where, away from your levels with the exception of  the TSLA entry. On the TSLA trade by the way I can see a V shape recovery on your chart before you entered short.  You said it yourself on the TSLA trade you were anticipating the move. Watch the accelerator modules. Try to plan your trades in the pre-market and focus on those and wait for entries/reaction at your key levels. author: EeveeWinter
content:
Even on a Monday, theta decay is already in play, especially for short-dated options like weeklies. Every passing hour erodes some value, particularly if the trade stalls or chops sideways — which TSLA apparently did between 10:11 and 12:19.
Even if the expiry is 4 days out, you’re trading the “front week,” so theta is not forgiving, particularly after the first push. That stall from 10:11–12:19 allowed theta to work against you.

content: This trade was a scalp based on the relative strength TSLA was showing. Gapped up in the pre market above a key HTF zone, retested and held the zone. author: 305 Trader
content:
manny, Good morning. When you trade the market open and until 10:30 am, you need to have a narrative beforehand that you formed off the HTF. There is a video for this from Jack. 

Now, if you trade after 10:45 am, having a narrative is not as necessary but rather studying the patterns the market made in intraday. 

Everything is about identifying patterns of price structure, also study videos on market structure from Jack (2nd video). 

When you trade the 1st hour in my opinion is more difficult because you need to have a good preparation of the HTF narrative. When you are newer this takes time to understand. And losses are more often as a result.

We trade patterns and when these patterns occur, we act. B&R is a requisite for entry but not a strategy in itself. After 10:30 am these patterns often become more clear. 

I cant tell you what period of the morning to trade or even the afternoon. But what I can tell you is that I needed to learn and still mastering two fundamental aspects of trading, namely

- Market structure: The gradual formation of higher lows that make new highs (calls). And the gradual formation of lower highs that create new lows (puts). Beware, this is the exact order I need to study them, not other way. 
When/where is that pattern broken? And when/where is that pattern in continuation? Two key questions to constantly ask when looking at your entry chart and one HTF above it. 

- Draw of Key levels or levels of confluence. A 5 min ORB is only one of them.  Also there is a video about it from Jack. These key levels represent in the chart where liquidity sits. You dont need to know the concept of liquidity, it is its force of reaction what matters. That reaction is what takes place in and around those key levels. This is why we draw them. What we are doing is identifying liquidity without some of us knowing we are. 

This is all you need, and master everyday everyday these two aspects.

author: FierceTiger

Please learn about liquidity levels and paper trade first if you must. It's a lot of money that you are just giving away to the market for free.

author: FierceTiger
content: Anyway, as a general rule, if premarket low gets taken out in the first few candles, you go long. If premarket high gets taken out in the first few candles, you go short.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Hi Max. Thanks for sharing your trades which lost, I think is brave of you to decide to do self accountabilty on your losses and to share. We learn a lot from our losses and other people’s.,I took the time to draw some thoughts and took screenshots of your charts. Then I draw where I would enter, not knowing anything about HTF and a narrative on those tickers, just purely intraday patterns from the chart.  I trade patterns. 

I know it may be a different style for you. I think the market always form patterns like a gradual ascending (long) staircase, or a gradual descending (short) staircase. These patterns repeat over and over intraday but it needs time to form. 

Trading the 1st 15 mins of market open in my opinion requires a very strong HTF narrative formed, a strong understanding of the stock and a good gut for being ok with accepting larger losses.  And this is because the market has not formed patterns yet, its only reaction to pre market and gaps. 
I trade differently. This is what I watch for, I follow a staircase formation which breaks price structure along  key levels of confluence and only act when a key level is respected as support or resistance to go in continuation. I think Neto trades this way more often than the other coaches. Its a matter of choice and backtesting. Very important this b/t part. 

Lastly, one advice, do not do initial mental stop loss. This is only for traders who have an extraordinary emotional management achieved and a rigurous discipline aka Jack or Tony.  I am not exaggerating. Our job is to defend our profits. The market’s is to provide the wins. 

Here is my screenshots of where I would have liked to enter safely.

author: FierceTiger
content: Price moves because it seeks liquidity. When PM high gets taken out, there is no more liquidity there and price moves in the direction where the next draw on liquidity is, which is a lower level. Hence you go short. I hope this helps.

author: FierceTiger
content: I only trade 1 strategy currently, and that is the "PO3 short strategy" at market open. Sniper entry, captured the big move. Quick exit. I have been consistently profitable for 2 weeks. Scarface taught me how to size up scale out efficiently and manage my trade on the 1 minute timeframe.

author: FierceTiger
content: Premarket high gets taken out, I short when market structure is confirmed, then I exit when momentum stops. You can see my earlier comments about premarket high for reference. I do actually trade this setup regularly.

author: FierceTiger
content: I entered as soon as I confirmed CISD on the 2 minute TF. My point of invalidation was high of day. Note that this is an A+ setup. Accumulation and manipulation was very clear from the pre-market - I only caught the distribution leg and rode it on the way down. Once this setup is formed, the chances of it reversing and stopping me out is extremely low. Also, note that you usually don't get a PO3 every time in the premarket with the premarket high getting taken out on the first few candles at market open. Sometimes PM high does not get taken out and it goes down anyway (this would not be A+, as it can reverse anytime). However once the PO3 short setup with PM high taken out presents itself, it is a bread-and-butter A+ setup.

author: FierceTiger
content: Can I ask what is your edge and what kind of setups do you trade? As for me, I learned that I do better and have a higher success rate in shorting rather than longing. The reason is I have trouble maintaining my position when going long and reading potential reversals (which can happen anytime). I tend to panic sell as soon as I see that first red candle, and can't hold longs for long. For shorts, it's usually an impulsive "elevator down" kind of move which makes reversals easier to read (i.e. when the move starts to lose momentum and has a change of market structure). So until that point, it's easier for me to hold onto my shorts to capture a bigger move. I sometimes trade obvious intraday reversals, but my current bread-and-butter and edge is shorting certain setups at market open and catching the initial move down.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi <@1398013674194468996> great question I often asked myself. It depends on how much attention and focus you need to take a trade. Think of it as a High-jump jumper in a track & field competition. Even though they are top performers, the tournament requires that they take a break in between their three attempts to make their best high jump. If you alread won in 1x trade, I would take a break and reflect on what happened, drink water. Only after my break I may come back to the charts. But now, think of actually the downside. and this one is critical. What do you do if you lose on the 1st trade? This is an even more interesting challenge, most people including some coaches here will be tempted to revenge trade and they will actually take more trades, with that loss in mind. This is a bigger mistake or offense to your own trading plan and system.

<@975051189316100096> Hi Chen. I thought I could share a thought on your trade. When taking a put or a call, wait for the set up that offers a larger displacement prior, so that your initial RvR is 1:2. 
I drew in  a blue box your relatively short displacement. 
Then in your 2nd entry for another put I guess, look at the strength of that pullback. Massive green candles that took out sellers. They even formed a higher high that took out your previous higher high which needed to be respected for the downward trend not to have a ChOCH. 
So your 2nd entry had very low prob of win and the fact that it worked it was to exit quick at LOD or the lower low of that dump.

hey Dane, I didn't take this but was watching it (short side is not my best side to put it mildly lol)

I'd suggest drawing out market structure (orderblocks) or HTF levels rather than just using the 5m level as your stop. 421.87 was last Fri low, and if you draw out at least the wick to the body portion of the 9:31 candle you can see the confluence.

content: Not only did I enter on the initial pullback from 9:30:48 to 9:31:33, I also entered based on the fact that I was also looking at HUGE numbers in the ASK side of L2 and L2 BID numbers for size were also at that time significantly smaller. It could possibly have a pullback after the initial open spike, or if it could continue to run... but as soon as it started to run I just kept my eye on the L2, Time & Sales, and price action. At that first real pullback at $297.50 the L2 was pretty flat, but QQQ still showed movement... so I just put my faith in the Q's and let it ride... while still holding my finger over the button to close the entire position. When it started to rebound, L2 showed the way, plus at that point it looked like a 1 candle rule entry, and Q's still looked OK so I continued to let the entire position ride. At the point I sold, I was looking at the ATH established last month, and when the price bobbled, I scaled out 80% and setup the sell for the remaining 20% if it ended up turning... holding my breath... as it went into consolidation. I had internally set my SL at $299, with finger on the trigger... and then... recovery and climbing. In the $302-$304 ranging, I didn't know if it was consolidation and futher climbing, or if it was done. It was trailers, and I was hoping to get over the $60k point for the trade, so I held... then it reversed and started to drop, and I closed out the final 20%. One hell of a ride! 😄

author: Das Wookie
content:
I broke a number of my rules today with my trades... not all of my rules, but quite a bunch of them:
- Don't trade after 11 - I couldn't resist the afternoon downturn to go short on PLTR and GOOG
- Don't revenge trade - I lost my first entry trying a trade with RGTI, so I exited the position, played a Uno Reverse card on my position and immediately re-entered, and lost again! -$4k on both. Doggie! That sucked!
- Don't chase! DON'T F-ING CHASE! Got it! - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't force trades - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Sit on your hands and WAIT for your entry - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't flit from one trade to another - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Max of 3 daily options trades... I entered in on 11 trades in total today
- Don't over trade - See max 3 trades
- If I do well on either of my initial 2 trades, and I'm green for the day... you're done! - I only had the two trades on TSLA and INTC, but I got greedy and wanted more. It worked out, but it shouldn't have. I got lucky that it worked out, but I SHOULD have just taken the initial win and shut down for the day and protected my wins. With the RGTI trade, I nearly went negative on all of my initial TSLA trade gains
- Capture gains at key levels and level out of position! - I ended up losing a potential $33k because I didn't take profit at a key level
- Don't move your stop! didn't exit at my initial stop, and then moved my stop, twice, trying to recapture the potential unrealized gain instead of just capturing the trade where my initial stop was set.

author: Das Wookie
content:
I generally am not, no. Sometimes I'll have it on in the background, just so I can listen in on what the boys are doing, but for the most part I'm doing my own thing. I trade more than B&R, and watch more symbols... so if the Mag 7 aren't doing anything interesting, and my thesis for the day ended up wrong... well then I'll just go wherever my watchlist leads me... and where one of my setups appears.

Today, it would have been VERY difficult to have not printed if you were still paying attention after 11, and the bottom of the bucket fell out for the next 2 hours. Today was an amazing velocity trade to the downside!!! This sorta day doesn't come around very often... at least not without some tweet from the pumpkin king that causes the entire market to panic. I was in a $40k trade last time that happened, and my jaw hit the floor!!! "Holy ****!!!! What the... What just happened!??? Babe! Pull up the news. Did the pentagon just explode or a dirty bomb go off in NY or LA????" as I watched TRILLIONS of dollars vaporize before our very eyes. I didn't have the emotional stability to cut my loss RIGHT THEN and switch to PUTS. I was too locked in on my CALL trade, and didn't want to cut the position... thinking "This HAS to come back! This is insane! What is the market doing?!!! C'Mon!!! Come back up! Please! PLEASE!!!" where as now, I'd just cut it. RIGHT then. Without Mercy! "It's just numbers." and these days, I'd just SCRAM the trade. That's also the basis for some of my rules. We all establish our own rules. Sometimes I break mine, but I do my level best to only do it for a VERY good reason. Sometimes tho, I slip up... like with my two RGTI trades today which was the basis of violating more than half of the rules I broke today. Of course, had I NOT broken that rule, and tried to chase a win there... I'd have already been out of the market and entirely missed this afternoons drop because I'd have been out of the market already and on to do other things.

author: Huy
content:
Seems like the average recommended position size to scale out is around 80% between TP 1 and 2. A few folks here would recommend only leaving a trailer if you have more than 5-6 cons but I personally think you can start with 3 for A+ set ups. Sell the first 2 cons early and leave the last one to see if it can ride the wave. The only downside is if the trade go against you then your gains from the first 2 cons will be wiped. Thats why I think 5 cons works better since you can sell 4 early then leave 1 to run. That 1 does not have enough weight to pull the prior profits if managed correctly. 

I am a big believer in actually doing. We can backtest / do simulation work / pretend how we scale in our minds etc but everything changes when you have real contracts and the tape is live. 

I just started to use this sheet here to help me quickly set my TP in the bracket order. The upside is I can lock in small consistent gains. The downside is I will miss on bigger moves on the first 80% of my position size. You can adjust the ATR to be more aggressive if you like which will change the values a bit.

author: DSmith
content: Hey everyone - I’m really looking for some recommendations going into next month and to get my mind right/refreshed. Tried that approach this week and obviously wasn’t a smart move…out of all weeks this one was the one where I should’ve just continued to scale at HOD. $4500 acct turned into $3400 in a week…and I’m definitely feeling a bit hesitant going into the next month if I stay on this trajectory. What have you guys done to get out of this situation and survive another day? Is the answer to scale? Or not to scale? 😂 I mean on MUs and SNDKs I’m basically just on 1 con, Fridays I’ve been downsizing, and then still get wrecked! I liked the setup that Jack took today on QQQ puts…actually got in like right before he did, and then the sucker just had the most massive green candle right after the entry…🤦. Maybe the lesson is just to completely avoid FOMC and earnings weeks all around. Here’s my month- AAPL on the 27th which was an amazing trade but all I had was -0.32 cents to show for it!😂 I appreciate any and all feedback!!

author: John H
content:
Quick post here regarding my oversized losses from Friday's session. I decided I would play WDC, and as we witnessed memory names had a brutal selloff. But more importantly what did I do wrong. I came in simply unprepared. WDC is not a ticker I normally trade. I had zero premarket prep. No key levels to watch and no exit plan when the trade turned against me. I failed to check a higher time frame to see that we were selling off. I should have marked off the open high low on the 5 min. candle as is often discussed here and I didn't do that either. So, an entire solid month of very good trading cost me more than 1/2 my gains for January. 

To make matters worse, I continued to trade. While I won most of those trades, I took yet another big loss on SNDK. I entered very late chasing more downside and the stock reversed near end of day. I'm saying this as a reminder to myself and for others that might be newer traders how very important it is to map out your game plan. To stay disciplined. To know your exit point if the trade turns against you. To not overtrade by taking too many trades, or revenge trade to try and recoup losses. Had I followed <@1014389514141184074> Friday advice I would of had a much better week & month. I was up $42K for January, I ended with $16K. I was disciplined all month long until Friday. That is truly how quickly you can make careless errors and give it all back. 

I still have a very high win rate, but you'll find that does not matter if you let trades get worse by the minute and don't get out when they are clearly not working. I will come to the market prepared, or I will not trade. That is something I have to follow for myself. We ride again Monday, and you all will see me turn this around. Best of luck everyone.

author: John H
content: One final thought. I would like to thank <@801169196015943731> for the amount of effort he puts into this discord. The daily premarket prep, the staying a few minutes after each trading session to answer questions. The detailed trade reviews win or lose. The time he dedicates to us on Saturdays to review trades and answer questions. This is a true coach & mentor we are lucky to have. I have not tuned in to a Saturday session in a while, and something today was telling me I should. I heard a lot of things I needed to hear again about trade management, sizing risk, and having a gameplan. Appreciate you Jack!

author: Fred
content:
Hi, here my trade for today. The result wasn't very good : two losses (-2.7R) and one win (+2R).
What's your opinion on the process ?

In 1h TF, we broke and retested the previous high from last Wednesday. Last Thursday, we broke the Wednesday low, which we then retested on Friday. The plan today was either a continuation of the bearish trend (break of the red zone/PDL) or a rejection of the previous low/PDH (red zone).

In 15m TF I identified 2 potential areas for a reverse (green area, which coincides with the red zone for rejection in 1h TF) and 1 area for continuation (red area).

On the 1m TF, the scenario is bullish; we disregard the bearish continuation opportunity and focus on the reversal. Based on my key zones identified on the 1-hour and 15-minute charts, I took puts on breakouts and retested previous 1-minute highs (market structure/yellow area).

author: Luke
content: Hey <@293563891345129472>, I really like the trade based on a "5min setup." As far as momentum on the 1min timeframe, you would like to have seen it work out right away rather than consolidating. After about 10:12am EST, I would have not been interested in it anymore based on the 1min timeframe since you would like to see it reject immediately especially for shorts. This is where you could go to the 5min timeframe if you have more of a bearish thesis and look to enter based off of the 5min timeframe. Also, another reason this would be a "5min setup" (you'll hear Jack and Tony refer to these as that) is you can see that there is a lot of displacement... in my opinion just a little too much for momentum shorts on the 1min timeframe for me, but based on the 5min chart it looks super nice. But besides that, your stop loss looks solid and your entry. This was definitely a trade you could have looked to re-enter with the 84% Rule on. Nice trade though!!

author: Joebag009
content:
The numbers: Cash account +64  Margin account:  -400
Take Profit Trader eval: -162 Top Step eval: -283 Apex evals: No trades

The Story
Lots of learning today. I’m not doing well personally, or in trading at the moment. Spilling my guts on here in a narrative others can comprehend instead of just short form writing to myself might help me work through it. I have done much better before so I know I can do this. 

Cash account: I have been trading in a margin account and sizing up worked great until my slump, since I have been trading 1-3 cons lately, I figured why not try a cash account with no margin? I took 720 bucks and put it in a cash account. I did one quick trade with JDUB on 2 0 day cons of NVDA and made 64 bucks. I could have held longer for continuation – I have gotten better, but still hold losers longer than winners. Losses happen faster than winners, cutting needs to be ruthless. I got lots of insight so my new rule for next week is trade 1 account only – cash if I can, margin if the trade of the day needs more capital. 

Margin account: I then had a thesis of continuation and a quick win in my margin account, but instead of waiting for a break and retest of that low of day on NVDA I entered too soon and it turned out being low of day. I stopped out on 2 NVDA 0 days for a loss of 314. Re-entering with hope of continuation and being wrong is something I am seeing as a problem. I will need to stick to the original setup and once I’m out, I’m out. If it costs me, I need to get better at exiting, instead of trying to re-enter. I also need to keep in mind 0 days move different. Looking at Fridays for the last 3 months I don’t see a meaningful performance difference from other days, so for now I’ll keep trading them.

author: Joebag009
content:
I keep getting to the half way point of passing evals and then getting close to blowing them, grinding up, then either failing with tight margin or a second tilt day. I wish I had tried props sooner, days of learning only costs me the eval fee. I wish more that I was passing them. I want to try trading multiple accounts at the same time with a copy trader for base hits that add up, I have two Apex accounts and wanted to link them to Ninja trader, only to find out that the feature was removed with the latest update to Ninja 8. I might try trading both together on Rthmic trader pro, but haven’t figured out the right settings and also need to tweak the master settings to switch from Ninja to Rthmic. I was super exited for Ninja with all the flashy Youtube shorts out there, it looks like ass out of the box and I am let down. So far my best results have been with TPT. Apex trailing drawdown scares me, I need 1 solid trade per day which might be good for my trading, but if I trail too close I’ll get stopped out in profit and then if I try to re-enter I’ll get cooked on small moves and spreads. If I size up I can’t give it too much room without blowing the account. Two micros and one trade attempt per day might be the best way to go, but how much is a healthy stop and healthy trail for Apex. I think Flowzone has a video on Apex best trading practice so I’ll check it out. I need to start trading the accounts next week so I can pass before they renew but this week has sucked and I’m not starting on a Friday.

Between all my accounts my challenges are: overtrading, tilt, sizing up instead of being consistent, not differentiating between good and crap setups, and rule breaking by staying on charts too long.

author: Joebag009
content:
Prop accounts: TPT -about 30 and 50 cents away from blowing it, will try a trade but fully expect to lose it and not planning to renew – will focus on my other accounts now that I have ruled out trading outside NY session.

TopStep: no trades.

Apex 02: +105 followed rules well
Apex 01: -264 slightly overtraded but caught myself and closed the charts before it got worse

Elite athletes don’t rise to the occasion; they fall to their level of training. -Neto Moreno

Theme of the day and Aha #1: SLOW THE HELL DOWN, be patient, DO NOT LONG OR SHORT IN CHOP, AT EDGE OF RANGES, AT HIGH OR LOW OF DAY… so in other words looking at a 1/5/15 min TF of a daily chart, the move looks HUGE, it makes us GREEDY, it makes us FEEL LIKE A BAD TRADER for “only” capturing xyz… but really, “perfect” is timing to SAFELY capture a relatively TINY part of that move with HIGH expectancy and CONSISTENCY. Doing it with 0s like JT (Jack and Tony) grants extra rewards.

Big aha for me today is how much more 0s can move compared to Friday expiries, 80% vs 20 profit roughly, however 0s are sooo spicy, even 1 option feels so high risk, definitely with the low discipline I have had lately. So, I could do 1 con 0 and be out when in profit, no re-entries ever on 0s, just with longer expiries, and not to mention that I’m super cooked if JT are wrong. I feel like I am at the beginning of my trading journey again, learning how to execute accurately and repeatedly with a single 0 will be a new challenge for me, I understand these are just for the main trade of the day and an A+ setup, I will want more wiggle room on most days until I’m consistently green.

author: Porter
content:
I should have mentioned this earlier, but I did **not** intend to use the 5-minute timeframe for the entry not on this trade itself.

* The trade was meant to be a **break and retest of the pre-market level (PML)**.
* The **5-minute level** was only being used as a **price target** in this situation.
* At the time, the ticker was **below both the PML and the PDL**, with **very bearish price action** from the prior day.
* Because of that, I felt using the **PML as resistance** made sense.
* Price had **wicked into the EMA and VWAP twice and rejected**, which reinforced that bias.

I should also clarify:

* I trade primarily on the **3-minute chart**, not the 1-minute.
* That said, after dropping down to the **1-minute**, I see what you mean about the entry — and you’re right.

Looking back at it now using **TradingView replay**:

* The **9:36 candle on the 3-minute** was the correct entry.
* I entered **too late**, mainly due to hesitation on my part.
* There also wasn’t any real **displacement** at the point where I entered.

Honestly, the only way this trade would’ve worked — now that I think about it — was:

* Entering on the **second retest of the wick during the formation of the 9:36 candle**.

One question for you:

* When price is **below both the PML and the PDL**, do you **prioritize the 5-minute levels**, or is that **circumstantial** for you as well?
* And you’re absolutely right about **shorting at the key level**.

Sorry — ChatGPT helped me write this 😅

---

author: Huy
content:
first candle rule (5 mins opening orb range) is something that is always good to map out and see so you can build some context. If the price is below both the PDL and PML, that would be one confluence for me. I like to take key levels into consideration such as the HTF levels. I am going to steal Jack's words but WMT offered that beautiful pop and fade today. 
PDL + 128.10 key support would have been a low risk entry. PML and PMH should be used with caution though since the levels created in this session does not have a lot of volume so its a thing we can look at if there's a HTF thesis and PM levels is the only thing that we can work off in a *trending* day.

if presented with this trade, I would only take a short off the 128.10 level that I mentioned either or go long if it can break and retest above 129.70 since this would violate the current structure and can shift for the upside. 

p.s. if you trade with wmt more in the future, you can watch the XLP index for another confluence

When market wants to sell off, the volume and intensity is strong so catching that move to the downside can offer nice rr's. Jack tend to be more aggressive when he find a bearish set up.

author: Kam
content:
I’d probably look at the 9:42 candle after the hammer at the 5m OR as the best retest. It’s aggressive (ahead of the 1m OB), but it has confluence with a prior daily level from the prior Fri high and is inline with the 1st pullback method

The 2nd and 3rd pulls look weaker to me because momentum hadn’t really established yet - GOOG was consolidating and the market was choppy. Retests tend to work best after clear displacement & quick pullbacks

IMO the 2nd best momentum retest came later around 11:20 on the 5m once relative strength was obvious.

Big thing though is replaying & practicing these moments in real-time speed. In hindsight a lot of things can look textbook, but we have to go with what we can see in the moment! Just because the 9:42 entry didn't go up & have continuation doesn't mean it was a bad trade according to the system. The 2nd & 3rd entries look good because they went up, but for every chart that looks like this, there's one that fails to the downside also

It's the exact same SL/TP only with a slightly earlier entry. During the same time QQQ was also moving up after creating a potential HL in the 1min.
https://www.tradingview.com/x/BURYd40B/

Earlier entry also means that the SL is lower. In this case the SL would have been just below the 5min range (so a reasonable SL compared to the first screenshot). No candle close below the SL and therefore this entry would have worked.
Such a "small" detail but in this case this led to a loosing day for me instead of a winning day. In options trading sometimes just a few seconds can make a huge difference. This is just another example for me that I need to be fast on my entries and avoid entries that are too late / too far away from the level. If however a late enry can not be avoided then my remainder is to simply use a wider stop which would have also worked in the nvidia set up. The learning/refining of ones playbook never stops xD

author: 305 Trader
content: Good morning. You were correct, the bottom was near but not quite at the time of your entry. It was at your exit.  Go in higher Time Frames, 4H and 1H with extended hours and find zones of solid confluence and draw them as rectangles as Jack does. Polish them in 15 mins TF around their edges. They will indicate bottoms and tops for larger moves. You need only few, maybe three max, 4. When the stock breaks those levels with a retest and making new higher lows (long) or new Lower highs (short) prior your entry, plus increasing volume and a solid reaction ( a hammer or a solid large bar),its a confirmation the reversal  or continuation is in place. This is how big moves are caught. If you only wait for this and do nothing, absolutely nothing else in your trading, you will transform your account. Then, study, study off time how the best break outs happen out of those levels and refine your entry. Go back in time in your Time frame of preference and one higher to study these breakouts from these zones you drew your zones exactly as I mentioned and study how the breakout took shape and what is the perfect set up for your entry. Last one, take this model and specialize in only one stock. Once you have dominated this (with $$$ to demonstrate it), you can add more stocks to your watch list. Of course, we often do what we want and think is correct, but if you would just listen to this advice, I promise you, you can get rich. This is exactly what I am doing now.

Hello,
on Friday my overall bias was correct because my bias was to the downside. QQQ dropped after market open and was below PDL but I still hesitated to go short on AMD. For me the learning is this (more like a "re-learning" because I should have known this): If Qs is dropping for about 30min without a significant pullback then the chance for a bigger pullback or a reversal is too high and therefore shorting stocks on the 1min TF is too high risk. Thus in future I would focus on shorting opportunities at the beginning of the session and avoid late short opportunities like AMD von Friday. (Based on all the trade reviews from Tony/Jack that I have watched I noticed that they never go short too late - like me in this case)

What are your thoughts on this? Do guys also avoid late entries or is there something else I missed? Thanks

author: Jdub
content:
Just caught back up with the chat.

I'd like to give my thought on some of the things said.

Taking signals is not a long term solution. I can promise you that if you solely rely on signals that you will lose in the long term and let me break it down. A great example of this is yesterday and today. Let's say you took the NVDA trade towards the downside yesterday. Most likely you would have sold on the first push towards the downside because you didn't understand the overall context of the trade. This was a higher timeframe thesis laid out in the pre market prep and we talked about this over the weekend as well. If the market is weak we will be look for NVDA towards the downside. And if you didn't understand this, you would have no conviction to hold for the move towards the downside even though there was a very good chance that it would head lower. 

And today for example lets say you missed the trade yesterday and now you got into NVDA this morning. I mentioned that its a completely different setup and that you have to keep a tight stop on this one as its more aggressive since its close to open. Once again if you didn't understand this it would be hard to capture. Price ended up rejecting off PML and reversing. When it came back up I stopped out quickly because I knew that price was most likely going to reverse. 

What I'm trying to say is that the problem with taking signals is that when you lose you end up taking a full R loss and sometimes even more since you will hold past your stop and when your in a winning trade you sell as soon as your in the green. You can't hold because you don't have conviction for yourself since your relying on someone else but yourself.

And that's just the reality of taking signals. That's why we teach to fish for yourselves. You think Neto became profitable by taking signals. He took some of the concepts that we teach and implemented it to his own trading, where he has his own system, strategy and edge in the markets. 

You saw my st

author: chunky_beast
content:
<@584423632802021376> I’ll keep this as simple as possible, but please what I’m about to say isn’t meant to be gospel. I don’t like leaving questions unanswered if I can help it. 

When you look at the upcoming earnings you obviously expect an uptick in options trading into that particular week volume wise but what will almost always increase is options holders/open interest (OI) into those options. Using AAPL for example we have a large disparity between Call holders vs Put holders for Nov 1 Weekly’s so with this we can estimate that, as of Friday, the market is currently expecting bullish move into earnings. Plus as you get from Earnings Whispers, on the wider options there was a large volume of Feb 21 265C bought on Friday indicating some bull expects a move towards the upside in the longer term worth putting up $12M. With all this you can infer the market a bullish outlook on AAPL. 

A different case is GOOGL where we can see, as of Friday, there isn’t really any high expectations for explosive short term moves based on next weeks options OI. 

There are other equities you can look at with extremely high bullish option sentiment like AMZN and AMD. 

However this is just a gauge, not a crystal ball. Do your own research and ensure that this correlates with your strategy and playbook. Also if you’re gonna trade shorter dated options into earnings this isn’t a swing where you may lose 60-70%. You can easily lose your equity so you have to be comfortable with a 100% risk to capital overnight.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Practical Use of Indicators
VWAP (Volume Weighted Average Price):
Tracks the average price based on volume and price action.

General guideline:
Buy calls above VWAP.
Buy puts below VWAP.

Helps determine whether the price is overextended or needs a pullback.
EMA9 and EMA21 (Exponential Moving Averages):

EMA9 is for short-term trends, while EMA21 represents medium-term trends.
Commonly used for pullback entries in trending markets, especially on the 5-minute chart.
Pullbacks to these levels often coincide with key support, resistance, or liquidity levels.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content: Order blocks and liquidity zones look different on 1-minute and 5-minute charts because they show price movements over different time periods. The 1-minute chart shows more small, short-term activity (noise), while the 5-minute chart combines those movements into bigger, clearer zones where major buyers or sellers are active. Use the 1-minute for quick trades and the 5-minute for spotting bigger trends and stronger levels

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
If you're a new trader or still working towards consistent daily trades, I strongly recommend starting with just 1-2 contracts to protect your capital. Focus on developing your own daily trading edge and strategy, as this is essential for long-term success.

Copying someone else’s trades might provide short-term results, but it’s not a sustainable approach. Instead, take the time to build your own foundation and confidence in your trading.

Additionally, PLEASE take the time to review the WHOP Accelerator Program and Tony/Jack's videos. They provide invaluable insights and teach you the essential steps needed to succeed in trading.

Make sure to identify the strategy that fits you best and stick with it. Remember, not everyone uses the same strategy, and trying to follow someone else’s approach may lead to confusion. Stay focused and committed to your personal trading journey!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Validate Trendline: Ensure it connects two or more significant swing lows/highs without cutting through candles. Retests confirm validity.
Trendline Break: Redraw if price closes decisively beyond the trendline with strong momentum or volume.
New Structures: Adjust for new formations, like flags or consolidations, as they develop.
Confluence Levels: Prioritize trendlines that align with key support, resistance, or Fibonacci levels.
Timeframe Matters: Short-term trendlines (1-min, 5-min) may require frequent adjustments due to volatility.
Price Behavior: Watch for tight consolidation or "riding" along the trendline as a signal for potential breakout.
Stay Flexible: Update trendlines dynamically based on real-time market changes and dominant structures.

author: Nines
content: This is essentially how you counter trend. Say your in a bearish market and you want to take a short but the next bearish key level is a bit far away and you dont wanna short at the current price because its over sold. You wait for order flow to switch from bearish to bullish on the 1h and 4h and then you take a long until that key level is met. Its crucial that you let a bottom form first and not just try and catch the bottom. Never ever try and predict the bottom u let it form and then play the counter trend

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@402084107951144973>  - 

🔍 1-Min Chart (Left Side)
More sensitive / reactive to short-term price movements.

The “Protect Your Asset” trendline on the 1-min chart is drawn tighter and shows earlier signs of reversal.

This chart captured a break of the downtrend earlier, showing bullish momentum forming right after Scale #3.

Best for quick scalps or precise entry/exit signals if you're managing a trade closely.

Shows micro price action and short-term exhaustion or trend shift faster.

⏱️ Use case: Ideal for day traders wanting to lock in profits quickly or protect positions once momentum shifts.


 5-Min Chart (Right Side)
Less sensitive and shows more confirmed structure.

The “Protect Your Asset” trendline here is wider and slower, but gives a stronger trend confirmation.

The trendline broke after Scale #3, confirming a larger shift in trend on a higher timeframe.

Gives more trustworthy signals when looking for bigger moves or validating a trend reversal.

Smoother and filters out noise compared to the 1-min.

⏱️ Use case: Ideal for swing entries, trailing stops, or holding positions longer with more confidence.

author: Kam
content:
hey there, while the key levels to take a trade may be different - the qualifying criteria for a break & retest at any level is the same (e.g. clear displacement; strong/weak price action at level, etc). 

using the 1m opening range is usually a more aggressive entry model, so if using the 1m OR we ideally like to look for confluences at that level (orderblocks or an opening play like a gap & go scenario, pre-market level, news, etc). 

Because of the big gap-down today and aggressive continued selling at the open, using a more aggressive entry model on shorts was necessary to try and capture continuation since in most gap-down scenarios we like to look for pops into higher timeframe levels before looking for setups - this generally leads to a higher probability & longer duration trades on the short side.

the best way to get deeper on this to to backtest and log some data to figure out which levels work best for you and on which tickers

also check out Tony's trade review on NVDA today; he goes over why the 1m opening range setup nicely after the gap-down with orderblock & PML confluence

<#1219022089252503632> 

 hope that helps!

author: Kam
content:
hey <@1323448182851637345>, I'm assuming your trade was a BnR off the 5m opening range. a few points to consider: 

1) on higher timeframes we opened up right in the middle of yesterday's range on the ES, so in order to go long or short at an intraday level like the 5m OR, you ideally need stronger evidence of momentum in either direction. Try zooming out to the 15m or 1hr timeframe to see the bigger picture 

2) the amount of displacement is key for the BnR system. In this case there wasn't a strong push or super strong candle close above the level you're using for the retest. Note the upper wicks (a tell for sellers) on the push above the 5m range on the 1m chart

3) if your entry was on the 9:57 candle, it's likely you didn't wait until the candle closed for confirmation that your level was holding. Remember you want to see strong price action at your level in order to enter. 

Will post a couple of links to videos Jack has made recently that may help with building higher timeframe narratives for your trade ideas as well as good entry confirmation signals

author: GoodVibez
content: Hey. Firstly, I feel your pain man. Gotta look at those higher time frames. Pretty much what Jack/Tony did. But remember they're not always correct in the immediate sense. Remember that huge TSLA swing  win Tony had. His orignial entry wasn't that great. It retraced down like $18. He bought more to offset while it worked back to original target. I'm not saying do that. Buy what did you see on higher timeframe that inspired you to enter Shorts? Also be mindful you only have until the June 6. Theta is gonna rip into the liquidity esp. as it sits negative. My experience...patience is good when on the the right side of a trade OR when you're intitally wrong, trust your thesis, and have bought enough time out (like 2 weeks to a month) for you to profit or at least break even. Maybe give it a lil time but you also don't wanna regret not cutting your losses at 3k when you're down $12k. Also think about the consolidation on the Daily chart. So if we're breaking above this level... it's probably gonna move with a little strength. very unlikely those puts will recover.

author: ajstrades
content: Ouch not sure what made you decide to short a SPY when it was making higher highs and higher lows since Fri on the shorter time frame. Have you done lots of back testing and paper trading? this kind of trading will have you blow up your account very quickly. Last thing you want to consider is going short when the market is making higher lows and higher highs. only way I would consider shorting SPY right now is a break below 575, otherwise it is in an uptrend. Follow the trend, the trend is your friend.

author: GoodVibez
content: At what Price point did you enter your shorts? This is something that I've kept in mind as I've experience a few big hit and have seen some comrades take some massive hits. This one thing... the market is predisposed to go higher. It's nature. We plant seeds so they can grow. Big Institutions want more, not less. So moves down aren't beneficial in the broader picture. I only enter puts in the short-term sense. IF it hits my stop loss or doesn't move towards my target with immediate conviction... I'm out that trade. I also don't leverage as much when entering shorts. Holding shorts for swings is a no-go in my rule book. I wait for big retracements or breakout levels and buy UP for time out. The likelihood your cons recover with longs/calls is higher. So the general consensus...CUT YOUR LOSSES, before the losses become too large for you to recover from. Can't recover if you don't have any funds to recover with. And the longer you wait... the more theta will eat your liquidity.

author: GoodVibez
content: So you added 20 more Puts to your position? Based on what information? What are you seeing on the charts to suggest doing this will profit? As you mentioned, you are acting on emotion. FOMO and revenge trading. You might get lucky, but my suggestion based on the charts and experience. CUT and reset your mind and come back fresh. Don't add to losing trades if you have no real overall thesis.

author: GoodVibez
content: Also I must emphasize, you have 2 things working heavily against you. Even if you're "right" THETA is going to kill you unless it moves with alot of momentum to the downside. If I looked correctely, the ones you added you only have until June 5 on. Brotha...

author: GoodVibez
content: Some ppl scalp in situations like this. Even the most experience traders don't catch the bulk of the move, even though it would be a dream. Many traders I know steer clear.  Issue with news driven price action is it really is more gambling. It can flip on you at any moment with serious strength. We can really put our account at risk. I did however catch a lil of this move to the downside. I looked at the Daily and Weekly chart and saw that they were void of candle body price action; gaps. I can see you have it on your charts as well. So I did a quick scalp. 4 cons at 312. Closed position at 309. Don't like leaving runners on Puts. So mentally, I think for me it's just a matter of 'Does what I'm seeing on charts support an entry and how comfortable am I losing the capital I'm risking if it hits my stoploss'. Hindsight is always interesting. I also chose to do 4 cons instead of 10, b/c like you, I was a tad apprehensive.

author: GoodVibez
content: I consider it every single time. Tony and Jack often mention sizing for zero. It's why strength/momentum is very important. I will often buy an extra week out just to help account for some of the loss due to theta if I see something on HTF, but it doesn't move right away and chops around, but never hits my stop. Yes, buying further out requires a little more of your capitial and puts more of it at risk should the trade move against you w/ lots of strength. Why we have stops though right. But the plus side, is that if you have conviction in your HTF thesis and just feel it needs a little more time to play out, it gives you the ability to hold a trade longer and not have Theta eat as much liquiditiy as fast. This may be beneficial for someone who doesn't have a margin account, has limited funds, and is limited the amount of day trades they can enter w/n a week. They don't have the luxury of opening and closing as many trades.

$NVDA - NVIDIA'S H20 CHIP SUPPLY CAN'T MATCH CHINA'S SOARING DEMAND: JEFFERIES

Jefferies says Nvidia’s H20 AI chip stockpile (600K–900K units) falls short of China’s demand, which could hit 1.8M units, following a temporary easing of U.S. export restrictions.

author: FierceTiger
content:
I am just going to share my trade thesis on TSLA: TSLA is a high-conviction intraday short if it becomes red (i.e. crosses previous day's close) tomorrow or anytime in the week, after this parabolic move up. It is the classic "First Red Day" setup. I think that the best way to play this setup is to short it with everything you got once price crosses from green to red, be out 80% of your position on the first green candle (first consolidation), and leave 20% runners (sell 10% EOD and leave 10% expecting Day 2 continuation). 
Point of invalidation (stop loss) is previous day's close, if it reclaims. But highly unlikely it reclaims on a "First Red Day" setup.

author: Markellwhite16
content: I see where you’re coming from. A couple  of things .. never predict . Our job is to react not predict. When you predict you go into the market with a bias and force trades. I am 100% bullish on PLTR until it breaks a key level on the HTF that causes a break of structure. You don’t wanna be the trader that keeps trying to short the highs and get smoked. The trend is your friend is a real thing. Look at names like MU , ORCl , even QQQ & SPY. Week in and out these names keep making new highs. PLTR can easily rip through its ATH , pull back , retest that level and continue higher. The last 2 days on this name has been a ROCKET 🚀! So my best advice is don’t go into the market trying to “predict” the exact top where a reversal might occur. Just keep taking what the market gives you.

author: Dora
content: Yes, I swing trade on occasion. I think it's easier because I usually have more conviction and I'm buying the options that expire much later. The DTE I enter depends on how long I think it will take the trade to reach my goal. Not sure of tutorials. On the daily chart in TradingView, using market structure info, I draw out those "long position" or "short position" tools to mark my stop loss and target. I usually enter into long (call) swing positions after a good company has had a bad day (fell by a lot), then start to go back up. This past year, I've swung ABBV, MELI, and LLY (most recently). I usually swing calls and seldom shorts.

author: Jonathan
content:
All losses teach something. If that NVDA trade still showed a 84% rule after losing a good chunck of % why would you enter again? Personally to me, even if the 84%rule shows itself if my loss has reached a point where i can't mentally handle. I do not enter and just wait for tomorrow. So you not entering that 84% trade can help you depending on how you see it or put into perspective. You already took a big L. Could you have handled another L if that 84%failed? 
Its good to disect every single loss trade and why they failed. 
What was your bias on the past trades you took? Calls or puts?  Ask yourself which ones favored you and which ones didn't. Calls,puts,tickers, time frames, even trading days. All those losing trades mean something about your psychology while trading.

author: JABARI
content:
Giving yourself 6 months to come to a conclusion like this isn’t realistic. Trading takes time, just like starting any other business or going to university to get a degree. It’s not a get rich quick scheme. I’m not sure what expectations you had coming into trading, but even trading 1-3 times a week already puts you at a bit of a disadvantage when you’re day trading. You need to be attending every trading day to build that momentum.

As for the trades they take, the announce it as soon as they take it, but options move very quickly and put your entry at a disadvantage especially when you’re waiting for someone else to give you a signal through a livestream. On top of that your broker matters as well with how they’re filling your order. in regards to the candles, there are different entry criteria’s they’ve went through extensively and it’s the same one they follow, the difference is they’re way more experienced with candle formations, discretion, and their edge that they will enter sooner than you can think to enter. 
You can always compare your entry time to the livestream they upload and see if it lines up, and I can assure you it will line up. 

Another thing about tony making 25k+ is because of his position size and the way he scales those cons - you’re not able to do that with your account size and psychology at this time unfortunately. It’s better you continue practising with 3 cons and always leave the last one as a runner so you can build experience or you can always paper trade using more cons to practise scaling.

Stay consistent, give yourself more room and time and be optimistic.

author: Dan Grand
content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share something important that I realized about my trading journey, and I’m hoping to get some guidance and support from the community.

Back in August, I had an amazing month trading options — one of my best ever. That gave me the confidence to try trading with real money. My first week was +$800, the second week I lost -$1000, and that’s when I realized I didn’t truly understand risk the way I thought I did. I stepped back and tried futures for a while, but trading futures alone without a mentor has been difficult. Watching YouTube isn’t enough when you don’t have someone to ask real questions.

And now, after thinking deeply, I’ve decided not to give up on options. I want to continue trading options seriously, because this is the environment where I feel the most support and structure, and where I’ve already seen my best results. I just want to do it the right way this time.

One big realization I had is this:

I can follow rules.
I can take clean entries.
I can respect my stop.
I can let my trades hit either TP or SL.

But what I cannot do well yet is read the higher-timeframe context and understand when the market wants to move long and when it wants to move short. That’s the piece I’ve never deeply worked on.

Sometimes I take a valid entry according to my rules, but I’m trading it completely against the larger direction — while some of you or the mentors are actually looking for trades in the opposite direction because of context. This made me realize I’m not just “pressing buttons.” I’m missing a whole dimension of the market.

So as I return to trading options, I really want to improve this part of my trading.
If anyone can share advice or help me build a simple, structured plan for learning how to read higher-timeframe context, it would mean a lot.

Thanks in advance for any support or suggestions. I appreciate this community and I’m ready to grow properly this time.

author: Luke
content:
I agree, it is difficult to get used to quickly calculating risk with the long/short position tool and using delta with it to get an estimate. For me, I always define my risk as a range of price; say I wanted to risk $100-$150 on a trade. Of course with options you can never have an exact amount but you can have a good idea. The delta on the ATM Calls on AMZN today was around .50 delta, and I was looking to risk around .50 on the underlying chart. I would multiply .50 by .50 and get .25 meaning you're risking $25 on each contract. Taking slippage and potentially stopping out a bit below the stop loss, I would take 4 or 5 contracts (risking $100-$140ish).
Hopefully that makes sense, that's just how I've done it and it works well for me

author: Huy
content:
just based on the way you ask these questions I can tell you are feeling really down..probably at the lowest point. I was there when I joined 2 months ago so I know exactly how it is. Here's my recommendation;
- take a few days off and just listen in. Ask Jack and Tony questions in the chat during the live trade if you have any. Stay calm; you would be very surprised on what you can see
- paper trade and or set the long / short position tool and pretend-trade to see how you do
- if you must take a live trade then go with just 1 contract for now
    - if you understand greeks and how it affects the premium; perhaps avoid OTM but rather go deeper ITM. This is because options are less sensitive to the underlying. A .7 / .8 delta will move more like a stock (just make sure the deep ITM options is liquid and the spread isn't too wide)

author: Pucon737
content:
I've just started live trading about two weeks ago and am still working to find my rhythm and edge. Confluences are really important for building confidence in setups, so I wanted to ask if anyone here has tried or had success with something like this:Add VWAP to your chart.
Go to the indicator's style/settings and uncheck (hide) the upper band, lower band, and band fill to keep the chart clean.
Strict rule: No calls (longs/bullish trades) if price is below VWAP.
No puts (shorts/bearish trades) if price is above VWAP.

author: Hugo Comte
content:
Hello,

Could someone share their Stats for Reclaim entry to the downside ?

After crunching data:

Reclaim entry PUTS: 4/4 looser. 0% win rate basically 
Reclaim entry CALLS: 1/9 looser: 88% win rate 

This is on the period since October ( as my trading was too poor before to generate useful data ) 

As per my data ( yes the sample size is small but still, its pretty contrasting ) , the reclaim entry doesn't work on the downside. We saw it very well on friday with TSLA.
If we had waited for a break and retest, there were no entry > no loss.

Curious to hear if someone can confirm with their data ( or show the contrary )

<@717554586843611217> <@801169196015943731> You have the largest sample size here, it would be helpfull to get your data in order to confirm or rule out this entry to the downside.

author: 305 Trader
content: Angel, hi. What do you trade? 0DTE or >1DTE? This is the first consideration. Theres is a huge difference in the way to trade. And this is something is not being taught. In order to make it as a 0DTE trader, you have to lose small and exit as soon as the trade is not taking off. Then, observe the PA and if its still in your favor, consider reenter. Also, you need abundant confirmation, which is what <@1325884177291280475> does and teaches. Also <@801169196015943731> and he calls it a “conservative” approach in his videos. What Tony teaches to exit in the closed candle and that “tails do the damage” is in my view as a 0DTE trader only, only to be implemented when you are trading >1DTE. Why? In 0DTE there is bo intrinsic value of the contracts, their value is only extrinsic, which means, they go up (or down) only because of volatility and time decay. The more Out of the money you are, is even worst for options, that is also another consideration. Options are not straightforward. Suggest you become familiar with IV% and its actual pattern during the trading day. Enter only when IV is rising for longs and puts. But this requires, very tight really tight stop losses for 0DTEs.

author: JP400
content:
Mess up today. Took AMD today PMH at 10:01 with 2 Cons. Looking for 2:1. Stop at PMH. Did not get the 2:1. Got scared when it came back and sold 1 Con to minimize loss. Then it stepped up again, so I bought another. Sold 1 Con at the close of 10:05. Pushed again. Held for 2:1. Did not get it. Then crossed my fingers (that well known strategy). Then sold last Con at 10:13 with QQQ selling too.
Tried AMD again at 10:14... (with QQQ above PDH). Lost again.
Tried AMD again at 10:22... (with QQQ above PDH). Lost again.
Then recovered some on 2 QQQ Puts off PML at 10:57. Caught that drop. Nice. And recovered my losses. Green day.
But I didn't stop trading !
I bought 2 QQQ again at 11:05 for a drop to PDL.  But it reversed, and I paid the price on the fast price changes on Friday Puts. Wiped out more gains.
And then... Took 2 more QQQ trades (2 Cons each time) to try to recover. Clawed some back. But just reinforcing terrible habits !
Overall loss on the day.

Sigh... Too tired after a busy day repairing cars yesterday. Wanted to make some money. Forgot the No.1 rule that it's not about making money. Broke my 11am rule, and did not stop at 11am. And also took waaaaaay too many trades.
Sigh...

author: MambaTrades
content:
One of my biggest issues the past 2-3 weeks of trading was being married to my daily bias and not being adaptive to change in price action and volume. 

For example, we've had a lot of gap downs and gap fills up and I kept trying to short the move up because I was married with the higher timeframe bias/narrative. Not good for this current market conditions with all the news, tariffs, and dip buyers. 

Moral of the story is be flexible and be willing to adapt your bias if price tells you a different story. 

Have a great trading day @here
